Lecture 10

Industrial Reactor

Assoc. Prof. Dr. Apinan Soottitantawat
Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering
Chulalongkorn University  ·  apinan.s@chula.ac.th
Adapted from materials of Dr. Jirdsak Tscheikuna, with permission

Five guiding questions — from lab bench to plant reactor

What this lecture answers

10-1What is the difference between a lab / pilot-scale and an industrial reactor?→ 10-2How do we specify the reactor geometry (from the reaction phases)?→ 10-3How do we specify the mode of operation (batch vs continuous, residence time)?→ 10-4What types of thermal operation (heat-transfer hardware) are available?→ 10-5How do we specify the thermal operation and size the reactor?→
10-1

Lecture 10-1

What is the difference between a lab / pilot-scale reactor and an industrial reactor?

Lab-scale reactor · 1

Batch stirred-tank reactor

Idealised — no transport resistance

Well mixed (no momentum-transfer resistance) · no mass-transfer resistance · no heat-transfer resistance. The bench reactor is built so that chemistry alone is measured.

Purpose: obtain concentration vs time, the rate of reaction, and a reaction mechanism / understanding.

Lab-scale reactor · 2

Tubular flow reactor

Closer to the real reactor

Runs at steady state, so it exposes catalyst life / deactivation and begins to show mass- and heat-transfer resistance — effects a batch flask cannot capture.

Gives steady-state concentration along the bed, plus catalyst-life data over long runs.

Scale-up changes everything

The industrial reactor — physical rates take over

Larger size

Volumes of many m³. Surface area grows more slowly than volume, so heat removal per unit volume falls.

Momentum transfer

Mixing is no longer instantaneous → a residence-time distribution replaces the ideal.

Heat transfer

Heat of reaction must be added / removed through finite area → sets the thermal operation.

Mass transfer

Interphase and pore diffusion may become rate-controlling — the reaction can be physically limited.

Industrial reactors may be physical-rate controlled (mass / heat / momentum transfer limited) — not just chemistry.

The scale of a plant reactor

Industrial reactors in the field

The design checklist

Information required to design an industrial reactor

  • Type of reaction — homogeneous / heterogeneous
  • Heat of reaction (exo- or endothermic, magnitude)
  • Kinetics of the reaction (rate law, parameters)
  • Residence-time distribution of fluid in the reactor
  • Earliness or lateness of fluid mixing
  • Stage of aggregation (micro- vs macro-fluid)
  • Catalyst behaviour — activity, deactivation, life
  • Phase equilibria and physical properties

These four questions (10-2 to 10-5) turn this checklist into a real reactor: geometry → mode → thermal hardware → thermal spec.

Interactive · why size matters

Surface-to-volume ratio collapses on scale-up

Slider spans 1 L (bench) → 200 m³ (plant). A/V for a spherical-equivalent vessel is $A/V = 6/D$.

4.84
heat-transfer area per volume  (m²/m³)

Interactive · reaction vs transport

Which resistance controls? Compare characteristic times

Reaction time is fixed by chemistry; mixing and heat-removal times grow with size. When either exceeds the reaction time, the reactor becomes physical-rate limited.

10-2

Lecture 10-2

How do we specify the reactor geometry — starting from the phases of the reaction?

The organising principle

The phases present decide the geometry

ClassPhasesTypical geometry
Homogeneous1 phase (L or G)Stirred tank (L) · tubular (G)
Heterogeneous — 2φL–G, G–L, L–L, S–L, S(cat)–GColumns, sparged / slurry tanks, fixed / fluidised / moving beds
Heterogeneous — 3φS(cat)–L–GSlurry, trickle bed, fluidised slurry, air-lift
Heterogeneous — 4φS–L–L–GMulti-impeller slurry tank

Reactor geometry & mixing arrangement — after Ray, S. & Das, G. (2020), Process Equipment and Plant Design, Elsevier.

Heterogeneous · single phase

Homogeneous reactions

Liquid phase

Stirred-tank reactors — batch / semi-batch / CSTR. For highly viscous liquids, a tubular reactor with a static mixer supplies the mixing.

Gas phase

Tubular reactors — plug-flow pipes / coils. Fast gas kinetics and short residence times favour a simple flow tube (e.g. thermal cracking).

Single phase ⇒ no interphase transport; geometry is chosen for mixing and residence time only.

Homogeneous · mixing hardware

Stirred tanks, static mixers & tubes

Heterogeneous · two phases · fluid–fluid

Contacting two fluid phases

Liquid in gas

Spray / tower (column) reactor — liquid dispersed as droplets into a rising gas.

Gas in liquid

Bubble column or sparged stirred tank — gas bubbled through the liquid (batch/semi/CSTR).

Liquid in liquid

High-shear stirred tank for immiscible liquids — e.g. biodiesel production (oil + methanol).

Key requirement

Maximise interfacial area so interphase mass transfer keeps up with reaction.

Heterogeneous 2φ · fluid–fluid hardware

Contacting two fluid phases

Heterogeneous · two phases · solid–liquid

Solid in liquid

Slurry stirred tank

Fine solid suspended in liquid — batch/semi/CSTR. e.g. PVC polymerisation (4–200 m³ vessels).

Loop reactor

Liquid fluidised bed with a pump circulating the slurry — PE / PP polymerisation.

Liquid fixed bed

Usually upflow so catalyst stays fully soaked, avoiding gas-filled dead zones — but abrasion / fluidisation must be avoided.

Why upflow

Complete wetting of catalyst; hydrodynamically stable operation window limits the velocity.

Heterogeneous 2φ · solid–liquid hardware

Slurry, loop & liquid-bed reactors

Heterogeneous · two phases · solid catalyst – gas

Fixed, fluidised & moving beds

Bed typeCatalyst lifetimeNotes & example
Fixed bedMonths – yearsVertical downflow (avoid fluidisation) or radial flow (low ΔP, large Δmoles) — e.g. styrene from ethylbenzene
Fluidised bedMinutesExcellent T control; catalyst circulates — e.g. acrylonitrile, phthalic anhydride
Moving bedSecondsContinuous catalyst make-up — FCC (fluid catalytic cracking), OCR hydrodesulfurisation

Low pressure is essential where the change in moles is large (e.g. dehydrogenation) — radial flow keeps ΔP small.

Heterogeneous 2φ · solid catalyst – gas

Fixed, fluidised & moving beds

Heterogeneous · why lifetime differs

Small vs large rate constant $k'$

Small $k'$ → reaction limited

Chemistry is the bottleneck · no reaction intermediate · very slow / no deactivation · long catalyst life → fixed bed.

Large $k'$ → physical-rate limited

Mass transfer is the bottleneck · reaction intermediate present · rapid deactivation · short catalyst life → fluidised / moving bed.

As $k'$ grows, the reaction outruns diffusion — the observed rate plateaus and the choice of bed follows the catalyst lifetime.

Heterogeneous · three phases · solid catalyst – liquid – gas

Why 3-phase reactors?

Slurry reactor

Fine catalyst in liquid with gas bubbles — e.g. oxidation of p-xylene to terephthalic acid.

Trickle bed

Catalyst wetted by liquid; gas & liquid flow (usually downflow) through the voids — hydroprocessing.

Fluidised-bed slurry

Catalyst suspended in liquid with gas bubbles — bubble-flow / air-lift reactor.

4-phase

S–L–L–G: multi-impeller slurry tank (e.g. PE production) — bottom impeller circulates, upper turbines disperse gas.

Heterogeneous 3φ / 4φ · hardware

Slurry, trickle bed, air-lift & multi-impeller

Worked example · a 3-phase system

Glycerol carbonate production

$\text{GLY} + \text{DMC} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{GLC} + 2\,\text{MeOH}$
  • Heterogeneous 3-phase: dimethyl carbonate (L) · glycerol (L) · solid catalyst (S)
  • Geometry → slurry stirred-tank reactor
GLY : DMC = 1 : 4 T = 60–80 °C V = 300 L Cat. = 3 %

Carried through 10-3 (mode) and 10-5 (thermal spec & sizing).

Interactive · phases → reactor

Pick the phases, get the geometry

Recommended geometry
—
Select a continuous and a dispersed phase.

Interactive · reaction vs mass transfer

Rate constant $k'$ sets the regime & the bed

Observed rate combines chemistry and external mass transfer: $\dfrac{1}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{1}{k'} + \dfrac{1}{k_c}$. As $k'$ rises, diffusion ($k_c$) caps the rate.

Interactive · axial vs radial flow

Pressure drop drives the flow arrangement

Ergun equation: $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\propto \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{\mu u}{d_p^2}$. Radial flow uses a short flow path (large area), so ΔP stays low even for small particles and large Δmoles (e.g. styrene from EB).

10-3

Lecture 10-3

How do we specify the mode of operation — batch or continuous, and the residence time?

The industrial vocabulary

Modes of reactor operation

  • B — Batch
  • SCST — Semi-continuous stirred tank
  • CST — Continuous stirred tank
  • FB — Fixed bed of catalyst
  • Furn. — Furnace (fired tube)
  • MT — Multitubular
  • FL — Fluidised-bed catalyst
  • TO — Tower
  • TU — Tubular

The industrial surveys tag each reaction with a mode + phases + catalyst + conditions + residence time / space velocity. The residence time is the single most useful selector.

Reading the industrial tables

Every process has a characteristic residence time

ProductTypePhaseT (°C)P (atm)Residence time / SV
Ethylene oxideFLG/S270–2901≈ 1 s
AcrylonitrileFLG/S40014.3 s
Ethylene (cracking)TUG550–7502–70.5–3 s
AspirinBL901> 1 h
CaprolactamCSTL80–11010.25–2 h
Nylon-6TUL/S260112 h

Gas-phase catalytic reactions: seconds. Liquid-phase / polymerisations: minutes to hours. This spread of 10⁵ in time sets the mode.

Rules of thumb

Residence time → reactor type

Long (> 1 h)

Batch / semi-batch reactors.

CSTR (< 1 h)

Continuous stirred tank for moderate residence times.

Fixed bed — LHSV

Liquid hourly space velocity 0.20–3 h⁻¹; use FB when LHSV > 0.20.

Fixed bed — GHSV / WHSV

GHSV 150–28 000 h⁻¹ (use FB > 150); WHSV 0.5–6 h⁻¹ (use FB > 0.5).

Space velocity $= \dfrac{\text{volumetric (or mass) feed rate}}{\text{catalyst volume (or mass)}}$; it is the inverse of a catalyst-based residence time.

Reactor choice · batch or semi-batch

When is a (semi-)batch reactor preferred?

  • To control the heat of a highly exothermic reaction (semi-batch: meter one reactant in slowly)
  • When side reactions form undesired by-products at high concentration
  • When reaction is instantaneous on contact (e.g. precipitation)
  • When equilibrium limits the reaction, or one reactant is only sparingly soluble in the other
  • When the reactant undergoes a sequence of reactions (e.g. pharmaceuticals)

Batch & Semi-batch Reactors — Worstell (2015), Elsevier.

Process-level decision

Batch or continuous flow process?

Default = continuous

Most chemical processes are designed to run continuously. Batch is the exception, chosen for specific reasons.

Consider batch when…

Production < 500 t/yr (usually batch) · < 5 000 t/yr (sometimes) · multiproduct plants · high product quality.

  • Scale-up problems · very long reaction times · handling slurries at low flow · rapidly fouling materials
  • Market forces · seasonal / feedstock-limited production · short product lifetime

Worked example · glycerol carbonate

Effect of temperature on batch conversion

t (min)60 °C70 °C75 °C80 °C
300.130.150.460.49
600.240.300.630.65
1200.380.620.880.89
2400.630.770.910.92
4800.880.880.910.92
$-r_1 = k'\,C_{GLY}$  (1st order)
T (°C)60707580
k′ (L/min·kg)0.550.691.821.87

Big jump in $k'$ between 70 and 75 °C — the rate constant, not just conversion, is temperature-sensitive.

Interactive · residence time → reactor

Where does your reaction fall?

Slider spans 1 s → ~11 days (log scale). The recommended mode follows the rules of thumb.

—

Interactive · the economic threshold

Batch or continuous?

Log scale: 10 → 1 000 000 t/yr.

Recommendation
—

Interactive · glycerol carbonate batch reactor

Batch virtual lab — find the time to 90 %

Model $x(t)=1-e^{-k_{app}t}$ with $k_{app}=k'M_{cat}/V$. Points = measured data at that temperature.

time to reach 90 % conversion (min)
10-4

Lecture 10-4

What types of thermal operation — heat-transfer hardware — are available?

The heat-management toolbox

Six ways to handle the heat of reaction

CaseStrategyHardware
1AdiabaticInsulated single bed / vessel — let T drift
2 – 3Isothermal / partial removalJacket, coil, a few tubes — internal heat exchange
4External heatAdiabatic beds in series with inter-stage cooling/heating; external loop
5External massDiluent / heat carrier, excess reactant, recycle, cold shots
6MultitubularCatalyst in many tubes cooled/heated like a shell-and-tube exchanger

Cases map to hardware differently for batch tanks and fixed-bed tubes — the next slides show each.

Batch stirred tank · Case 1-2-3-6

Internal heat exchange — jackets & coils

Simple / dimple jacket

Full-wall jacket; dimples add turbulence & rigidity. Moderate duty, whole vessel wall.

Half-pipe jacket

Welded half-pipe spiral — high pressure, high velocity coolant, good U.

Helical / internal coil

Extra area inside the liquid for large duties beyond the wall's capacity.

Baffle coil

Coils double as baffles — area + mixing together.

Wall area scales as $V^{2/3}$ while duty scales as $V$ — big vessels need coils, not just a jacket.

Case 1-2-3-6 · batch tank hardware

Jackets & coils on the real vessel

Batch stirred tank · Case 4 & Case 5

When the jacket is not enough

Case 4 — external heat

Pump the liquid through an external heat exchanger and return it (semi-batch with outside circulation) — e.g. PP polymerisation.

Case 5 — external mass

Remove heat by evaporating a component, condensing it outside, and returning the condensate (reflux cooling).

External loops decouple heat-transfer area from vessel size — the area is set by the exchanger, not the wall.

Tubular fixed bed · Case 1 & Case 2-3

From adiabatic to partial heat removal

Case 1 — adiabatic bed

Insulated single bed; inert packing at the inlet distributes feed, prevents catalyst movement & scale. Good for mild heats of reaction (e.g. mild hydrogenation).

Case 2-3 — isothermal / partial

A few cooling/heating tubes (basket-type design); not for high heats. Coolant (e.g. CW) in the tubes gives partial removal / near-isothermal.

Inert inlet packing: distributes feed · prevents catalyst movement · prevents scale · moderates the initial heat release.

Tubular fixed bed · Case 2-3-6

High heat duties: multitubular & direct-fired

Multitubular non-adiabatic

Catalyst in hundreds of 1–2″ tubes, shell-side coolant (boiling water, parallel or cross flow), $A_{max}\approx1000\ \text{m}^2$. Close T control for selectivity — EO, methanol → formaldehyde, phthalic anhydride.

Direct-fired non-adiabatic

Tubes in a furnace supply high-temperature heat for a rapid endothermic reaction — e.g. steam reforming.

Case 2-3-6 · fixed-bed hardware

Basket, multitubular & fired reactors

Tubular fixed bed · Case 4

Adiabatic beds in series with inter-stage cooling/heating

  • Splits a large heat load across several adiabatic beds
  • Cool (exo) or heat (endo) between beds — approach equilibrium in stages
  • Enables high conversion for equilibrium-limited reactions

Examples: SO₂ oxidation · hydrocracking · catalytic reforming · ammonia synthesis · styrene from ethylbenzene · methanol synthesis.

Case 4 · industrial examples

Staged adiabatic beds in industry

The governing equation

Energy balance for the industrial reactor

$\displaystyle \dot Q + \dot W_s - (-r_A)V(-\Delta H_{Rx}) = \sum_i F_i C_{p,i}\,(T - T_{feed})$

Steady state; shaft work neglected ($\dot W_s\approx0$). Heat generated by reaction either leaves through $\dot Q$ or raises the stream temperature.

$\dot Q<0$: remove heat (exothermic) $\dot Q=0$: adiabatic $\dot Q>0$: add heat (endothermic)

Case 5 · adding mass to carry heat

Extraneous component, excess reactant & cold shots

Extraneous component

Inert that is well-mixed, easily separated, has high heat capacity — a heat carrier, e.g. steam in styrene from EB (EB : steam ≈ 1 : 10).

Excess reactant / recycle

Preferred over an inert: recycle more reactant or a product to moderate the temperature change without a foreign species.

Cold shots

Inject cold inert / reactant between beds to quench — SO₃ synthesis (shallow beds, 1–2 s), hydroprocessing trickle beds.

Radial flow + quench

Quench feed at the inlet of a radial-flow fixed bed keeps ΔP and peak T down.

Case 5 · external-mass schemes

Excess reactant, diluent & cold shots

Interactive · can the jacket cope?

Heat duty outruns the jacket on scale-up

Wall area $\propto V^{2/3}$; reaction duty $\propto V$. Jacket $Q=UA\,\Delta T$. When the required duty exceeds the jacket, add an internal coil or external loop.

Interactive · equilibrium staircase

Staging + inter-cooling climbs toward equilibrium

Exothermic reversible reaction (e.g. SO₂ oxidation / ammonia). Each bed follows an adiabatic line (T rises, X rises) up toward the falling equilibrium curve; inter-cooling drops T at constant X so the next bed can go further.

final conversion after the last bed

Interactive · steam as a heat carrier

A high-Cp diluent moderates the temperature swing

Adiabatic swing $|\Delta T_{ad}| = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_{Rx})\,F_{A0}\,X}{\sum_i F_i C_{p,i}}$. Adding inert raises $\sum F_iC_{p,i}$, shrinking the swing — the EB→styrene reactor runs steam : EB ≈ 10 : 1.

adiabatic temperature swing |ΔT| (°C)
10-5

Lecture 10-5

How do we specify the thermal operation — and size the reactor?

The selection logic

Choosing the thermal operation, step by step

Case 1 · Adiabatic
Exo: $T_{out}T_{min}$ & $T_{out}>0.85\,T_{in}$
Case 2 · Isothermal
Tubular: $\dot Q<2500$ kW, $A<1000\ \text{m}^2$ · Tank: $\dot Q<700$ kW, $A<50\ \text{m}^2$
Case 3 · Partial removal
Single reactor, partly heat removing / adding
Case 4 · Beds in series
Adiabatic beds + inter-cool/heat, $T_{out}-T_{in}<0.15\,T_{in}$; or batch with external heating/cooling
Case 5 · External mass
Heat carrier · excess reactant · recycle · inert · cold/hot shot
Case 6 · Multiple isothermal
Parallel units — repeating / numbering scale-up (non-economic, last resort)

Glycerol carbonate · the data for design

Arrhenius rate constant & heat of reaction

$k_1 = k_{10}\exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0}-\dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$
k₁₀ = 1.82 @ 75 °CE₁ = 66.49 kJ/mol

Fitted from the batch data — but may still hide external/internal mass-transfer effects at high T.

$\Delta H_{Rx}=\Delta H_{Rx}^{0}+\Delta C_p\,(T-T_R)$
SpeciesCp (kJ/mol·K)ΔH°f (kJ/mol)
DMC0.157−607
Glycerol0.201−667
Glycerol carbonate0.198−788
Methanol0.104−238

$\Delta H_{Rx}^{0}=+10.33$ kJ/mol → mildly endothermic.

Design run · Case 1 tested

Adiabatic CSTR — the temperature falls

Endothermic reaction with $\dot Q=0$ ⇒ the reaction consumes sensible heat, so the outlet cools from 75 °C to 61.9 °C.

$T = T_F - \dfrac{F_{A0}X(-\Delta H_{Rx})}{\sum_i F_i C_{p,i}}$
T = 61.9 °C k₁ = 0.74 (↓ from 1.82) M_cat = 11 344 kg LHSV = 5.9 h⁻¹

Lower T → lower $k_1$ → far more catalyst needed. Adiabatic is not acceptable here — the cooling kills the rate.

Design run · Case 2 chosen

Isothermal CSTR — supply heat, keep the rate

Hold 75 °C by adding $\dot Q=20\,059$ kJ/min. The rate constant stays at its full value and the catalyst charge drops sharply.

$\dot Q = F_{A0}X(-\Delta H_{Rx}) = 20\,059\ \text{kJ/min}$
T = 75 °C (held) k₁ = 1.82 M_cat = 4 605 kg LHSV = 14.56 h⁻¹ Q = +20 059 kJ/min

Isothermal ⇒ 2.5× less catalyst than adiabatic. Tank duty $\dot Q<700$ kW ⇒ a jacketed / coiled slurry CSTR is feasible.

Realising the isothermal tank

Selecting the jacket for the isothermal CSTR

Jacket options

Half-pipe coil · simple jacket · dimple jacket · internal coil — increasing heat-transfer area for larger duties.

Selection guideline

Chosen from tank size and pressure: small/low-P → simple jacket; higher-P / higher-duty → half-pipe or internal coil.

Chemical Process Engineering Design & Economics — H. Silla (2003).

Sizing & the workflow

Cost correlation & general design steps

$\log_{10}C_p = K_1 + K_2\log_{10}V + K_3(\log_{10}V)^2$
Reactor typeK₁V range (m³)F_BM
Jacketed agitated4.1050.1–354.0
Jacketed non-agitated3.3505–454.0
Autoclave4.5591–154.0
  • Use an apparent 1st-order rate for the first design pass (lab/pilot) and conceptual design
  • Validate & revise the rate expression at each scale
  • Use the rate from the largest pilot/lab scale to revise the commercial size
  • Add rate-expression detail only in the later design steps

Interactive · walk the decision tree

Find the thermal case for your reactor

Selected thermal operation
—

Interactive · glycerol carbonate CSTR

Adiabatic drift vs isothermal duty

Endothermic ($\Delta H_{Rx}\!\approx\!+12$ kJ/mol). Adiabatic: outlet T falls with X. Isothermal: T held at 75 °C but you must supply $\dot Q$. At X = 0.9 → T ≈ 62 °C or $\dot Q\approx20\,000$ kJ/min.

Interactive · why temperature matters

Temperature sensitivity of $k_1$

$k_1 = 1.82\exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_1}{R}\!\left(\dfrac1{T_0}-\dfrac1T\right)\right]$, $E_1=66.49$ kJ/mol, $T_0=75$ °C. A 14 °C drop (isothermal→adiabatic) already halves $k_1$.

k₁ (L/min·kg cat)
?

Check yourself

Six questions across the industrial-reactor design workflow.

Quiz · 10-1 & 10-2

Scale-up & geometry

Why can an industrial reactor be physical-rate controlled when the bench version of the same reaction is not?

A gas-phase solid-catalyst reaction has a large $k'$ and the catalyst deactivates in minutes. Which bed?

Quiz · 10-3 & 10-4

Mode & thermal hardware

A liquid reaction needs 6 h residence time at a production rate of 200 t/yr. Best mode?

An exothermic reversible reaction is equilibrium-limited but you need high conversion. Best thermal strategy?

Quiz · 10-5 & synthesis

Specifying the thermal operation

Run adiabatically, the endothermic glycerol-carbonate CSTR cools to 62 °C and needs 2.5× more catalyst. Best fix?

Steam is fed 10 : 1 with ethylbenzene in the styrene reactor. Its main role?

Exit ticket

Key Takeaways

1

A lab reactor measures chemistry alone; the industrial reactor adds momentum, heat and mass-transfer resistances that grow with scale.

2

Design in four moves: geometry (from the phases) → mode (from residence time & production rate) → thermal hardware → thermal specification.

3

Residence time selects the mode: >1 h → batch, <1 h → CSTR, catalytic space velocity → fixed bed; <500 t/yr leans batch, >5000 t/yr continuous.

4

Match hardware to heat load: jacket → coil → multitubular → adiabatic beds + inter-cooling → external mass (diluent, cold shot) as duty rises.

5

Always test the energy balance: the endothermic CSTR that cools 13 °C adiabatically becomes practical only isothermal.
